revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23991619004|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:帝室博物館]]
'''Imperial Household Museums''' (帝室博物館, '''Teishitsu Hakubutsukan''') were museums under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] from the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} to the [[:en:Allied occupation of Japan|Allied occupation of Japan]]. They are the predecessors of today's '''{{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}''', '''{{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|qid=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}''', and '''{{ill|Nara National Museum|en|Nara National Museum|qid=Q147312|lt=Nara National Museum|1=Nara National Museum}}'''.
== Overview == === History === [[File:Tokyo Imperial Household Museum.JPG|thumb|250px|The former Tokyo Imperial Household Museum designed by Josiah Conder.]] The Imperial Household Museums originated from the "Exposition Bureau" established in February 1872 (Meiji 5) by the [[Grand Council of State]] at the former Honda residence site near the Hibiya Gate. This bureau served as a preparatory body for the [[Vienna World's Fair]]. In March 1873, the Exposition Bureau integrated the museum, library, and botanical garden under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science and Culture (Japan)|Ministry of Education]], forming departments for botany (plants, animals, and minerals), archaeology (antiquities and books), industry (machines and production), and general affairs (translation and secretarial work). Japan's participation in the World's Fair began that May.
After the exposition, the Bureau underwent reorganization, becoming part of the {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|qid=Q1142265|lt=Home Ministry|1=Home Ministry}} in March 1875 and renamed the "Museum." By January 1876, it was further restructured, leading to specialization in its divisions under the Home Ministry.
In April 1881, the museum transferred to the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|en|Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|qid=Q3315480|lt=Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce|1=Ministry of Agriculture and Commerce}}. By October 1883, the management of treasures from the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|qid=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin|1=Shōsōin}}—some displayed since 1875—was transferred to the Imperial Household Ministry. The museum itself followed in March 1886, becoming part of the Imperial Household Ministry's Library Department in 1888. Staff were dispatched to regional sites for the evaluation and preservation of artifacts.
On May 16, 1889, three museums—Imperial Museums in Tokyo, Kyoto, and Nara—were officially established. These institutions expanded the scope of museum management to Kyoto and Nara, with their openings occurring in 1897 and 1895, respectively. Prominent figures like {{ill|Okakura Kakuzō|ja|岡倉覚三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okakura Kakuzō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岡倉天心|1=Okakura Kakuzō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q942995}} (Tenshin) and {{ill|Ernest Fenollosa|en|Ernest Fenollosa|qid=Q933253|lt=Ernest Fenollosa|1=Ernest Fenollosa}}, who were pivotal in Japan’s art scene, contributed to their development.
From 1900, these institutions were renamed the '''Tokyo Imperial Household Museum''', '''Kyoto Imperial Household Museum''', and '''Nara Imperial Household Museum'''. The museums retained this name until 1947.
[[File:Hyokeikan - Tokyo National Museum.jpg|thumb|250px|Hyokeikan, Tokyo National Museum.]] The construction of Hyokeikan, an exhibition hall in the Ueno complex, was completed in 1909 to commemorate the wedding of the [[:en:Taishō Emperor|Taishō Emperor]]. The Neo-Baroque building was designed by court architect {{ill|Katayama Tōkuma|en|Katayama Tōkuma|qid=Q898689|lt=Katayama Tōkuma|1=Katayama Tōkuma}}.
During the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|qid=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}}, the Kyoto Imperial Household Museum transferred to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}} in 1924, becoming the '''{{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|恩賜京都博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto National Museum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都国立博物館|1=Kyoto National Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q147286}}'''. In 1947, the Tokyo and Nara museums were reorganized into the '''{{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}''' and '''{{ill|Nara National Museum|en|Nara National Museum|qid=Q147312|lt=Nara National Museum|1=Nara National Museum}}''', respectively.
=== Reconstruction after the Great Kantō Earthquake === The 1923 {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|en|Great Kantō Earthquake|qid=Q274498|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|1=Great Kantō Earthquake}} severely damaged the main building and annexes in Ueno. The reconstruction of the main building, led by architect {{ill|Jin Watanabe|en|Jin Watanabe|qid=Q62269907|lt=Jin Watanabe|1=Jin Watanabe}}, was completed in 1938. The building, featuring fireproof steel-reinforced concrete and a Japanese-style roof, remains a notable example of {{ill|Imperial Crown Style|en|Imperial Crown Style|qid=Q11060089|lt=Imperial Crown Style architecture|1=Imperial Crown Style}}.
== Facilities == === Main Building === [[File:TokyoNationalMuseum.jpg|thumb|280px|Tokyo National Museum Main Building.]] The present Tokyo National Museum main building, completed in 1937, is a steel-reinforced concrete structure designed by {{ill|Jin Watanabe (architect)|en|Jin Watanabe (architect)|qid=Q11562043|lt=Jin Watanabe|1=Jin Watanabe (architect)}}. It combines traditional Japanese and Western architectural elements. Declared an Important Cultural Property in 2001, it houses exhibitions of Japanese art, including paintings, sculptures, and crafts.
== Organization == Each Imperial Household Museum was overseen by a Superintendent, with a President stationed at the Tokyo Imperial Household Museum. Directors managed the Kyoto and Nara museums.
=== Past Presidents === 1. {{ill|Takuma Kotono|ja|股野琢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takuma Kotono|1=Matano Taku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q27921144}} (1900–1917) 2. {{ill|Mori Ōgai|en|Mori Ōgai|qid=Q356960|lt=Mori Ōgai|1=Mori Ōgai}} (1917–1922) 3. {{ill|Yonekichi Miyake|ja|三宅米吉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yonekichi Miyake|1=Yonekichi Miyake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11355076}} (1923) 4. {{ill|Yoshio Ōshima|ja|大島義脩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Ōshima|1=Yoshinaga Ōshima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11434961}} (1923–1932) 5. {{ill|Eizaburō Sugi|ja|杉栄三郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizaburō Sugi|1=Eizaburō Sugi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q27921165}} (1932–1939) 6. {{ill|Makoto Watanabe|ja|渡部信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Watanabe|1=Shin Watanabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q27920760}} (1939–1944) 7. {{ill|Masao Toki|ja|土岐政夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masao Toki|1=Masao Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q20041633}} (1944–1946) 8. {{ill|Yoshishige Abe|en|Yoshishige Abe|qid=Q11450340|lt=Abe Yoshinari|1=Yoshishige Abe}} (1946–1947)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Tokyo National Museum, ''120 Years of History and Art of Japan and Asia,'' 1992. * Tokyo National Museum, ''Visual History of 120 Years,'' 1992. * {{ill|Terunobu Fujimori|en|Terunobu Fujimori|qid=Q3134309|lt=Tetsuo Fujimori|1=Terunobu Fujimori}}, ''Modern Japanese Architecture,'' Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * ''Monthly Cultural Properties,'' Issue 349, Daiichi Hōki, 1992.
{{Imperial Household Ministry}} {{Museum-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|帝室博物館|version=104042451|comment=Imported full ja history}}
